content:
When Code Outpaces the People Who Are Supposed to Understand It

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

This article was originally written in my native language, Japanese, and has been translated into English by AI. You can read the original Japanese version here.
Software development has long rested on an unspoken assumption: a structure in which humans write the code, humans understand it, and humans maintain it.
Every stage built on top of this assumption — design, review, testing, operations. Code was an extension of human thought, and responsibility for it rested with humans as well.
But that outline is now quietly beginning to crumble.
From Something Written to Something Generated
The arrival of AI coding tools has introduced a subtle twist into this structure. Humans provide specifications in words, and AI fills in the implementation details. Code is no longer something you write — it’s becoming something generated.
What’s happening here isn’t simply a productivity gain. It’s a change in which the pathway to understanding gets short-circuited.
In the past, the act of writing code was itself the process of understanding. Through designing, hesitating, and rewriting, the structure of a system took root in the writer’s mind. AI, however, skips that intermediate process. As a result, code is produced faster, but what it means and what tradeoffs it rests on don’t necessarily register in the writer’s awareness.
Code That Runs, Understood by No One
What emerges from this is code that exists without ever being understood.
On the surface, it works. It might pass the tests. It might pass review. But layered inside it are design choices no one explicitly made, and assumptions no one ever explained. In fact, one survey found that 43% of AI-generated code changes require manual debugging in production even after passing QA and staging tests.
What matters is that the rate at which this code accumulates has begun to outpace the rate at which humans can understand it. Mario Zechner and Armin Ronacher, who built core parts of the popular AI coding agent OpenClaw, warned in a Wall Street Journal report that the industry has a problem worth calling “vibe slop.” Their point: more and more developers are letting AI skip the parts that actually matter — design, judgment, testing, ownership, and deep understanding of the system. The warning carries extra weight because it comes from the very people who built the tools, sounding the alarm from the inside.
Google CEO Sundar Pichai has said that roughly 75% of the company’s new code is now generated by AI. The sense that code is shifting from something written to something generated is no longer confined to a handful of workplaces.
The Collapse of the Assumption Is Not a Handoff to AI
This is where the old assumption — humans understand it, humans maintain it — begins to waver.
But the collapse of this assumption doesn’t simply mean that “AI understands instead.” The situation is more complicated than that. AI can generate code, but it remains an incomplete agent when it comes to autonomously guaranteeing the meaning of that code as a whole, or the structure of responsibility behind it.
What results is a codebase in which the subject of understanding has grown diluted.
Humans can’t fully understand the whole. AI takes no responsibility for the whole. And in between, the code just keeps multiplying.
Redefining the Concept of “Understanding”
Still, it would be insufficient to treat this shift as nothing more than a crisis.
What’s actually happening is less that the old assumption is collapsing and more that the concept of understanding itself is being redefined. No single person needs to understand everything anymore — but in exchange, the system is required to possess understandability in some other form.
Software engineering researcher Margaret-Anne Storey argues in a paper that the shared understanding within a team — the thing that made it safe to change a system — is quietly eroding in the age of AI. She calls this loss of understanding “cognitive debt,” and the failure to record intent and reasoning “intent debt,” framing both as debts that now rival, or even exceed, technical debt in importance. Her view is that externalizing understanding and intent, rather than leaving them locked inside individual minds, is itself a way to reduce that debt.
That might mean making specifications explicit rather than relying on the code alone. It might mean locking down behavior through tests. It might mean explainability through logs and auditability. Or it might mean a dynamic verification process built on collaboration between humans and AI.
The Question Isn’t Who Understands
In other words, the essence of the problem is no longer “who understands it.”
What’s being asked instead is this:
Can this system remain verifiable as a process — not as something that lives inside any one person’s head?
Closing Thoughts
The assumption that humans understand and humans maintain is quietly coming to an end. But that ending is arriving less as chaos and more as an update to our design philosophy.
Understanding is no longer a personal capability — it becomes a property distributed across the entire system. And maintenance shifts from the act of finding someone who understands to the act of designing for understandability itself.
Perhaps, in the middle of this transition, we are still just trying to describe a new phenomenon with old words.
